q: Which of the following statements correctly describe the characteristics and methods of a 'Collection' in IB Pseudocode?
right: It is a dynamic data structure that can grow or shrink in size.
The .getNext() method returns the next item and moves the pointer forward.
The .hasNext() method checks if there is another element to read.
It is often described as an unordered 'bag' of data.
The .resetNext() method must be called to start reading from the beginning.
It does not support direct access via an index like [i].
wrong: It has a fixed size defined at the start of the program.
You can use a 'for' loop with an index to access elements.
The .length() method is used to find the total number of items.
Items can only be removed from the front of the collection.
It is a static data structure stored in contiguous memory.
The .addItem() method requires an index as a parameter.

q: Identify the true characteristics and applications of a Stack data structure.
right: It follows the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle.
The 'pop' operation removes the most recently added item.
It is used to manage return addresses during sub-routine calls.
The 'push' operation adds a new element to the top of the stack.
It is used by web browsers to manage the 'Back' button history.
A stack overflow occurs if you push to a full stack.
wrong: It follows the First-In, First-Out (FIFO) principle.
The 'dequeue' operation is used to remove the top element.
Items are added to the 'tail' and removed from the 'head'.
It is the primary structure used for print job buffers.
Random access allows any element to be removed at any time.
It is more efficient than an array for searching specific values.

q: What are the defining features and use-cases for a Queue data structure?
right: It operates on a First-In, First-Out (FIFO) basis.
The 'enqueue' operation adds an element to the back (tail).
The 'dequeue' operation removes an element from the front (head).
It is used in IO buffers to manage data transfer speeds.
A printer spooler uses a queue to order documents.
It maintains the chronological order of data arrival.
wrong: The last item added is the first one to be removed.
It uses 'push' and 'pop' as its primary methods.
It is typically used to implement 'Undo' functionality in software.
The pointer always resets to the middle of the structure.
Elements are removed from the tail and added to the head.
It is a non-linear data structure like a tree.

q: In the context of 'Thinking Ahead', what are the requirements and purposes of pre-conditions and post-conditions?
right: Pre-conditions define the state required before a method starts.
Post-conditions describe the state of the system after execution.
They help reduce the need for excessive error-checking within code.
They act as a contract between the programmer and the user.
Pre-conditions often specify the data types of input parameters.
Post-conditions ensure the expected output or side effect is achieved.
wrong: Pre-conditions are only checked while the program is running.
Post-conditions are used to determine the Big O complexity.
Pre-conditions are the same as loops and conditional checks.
They are only necessary for recursive algorithms.
Post-conditions define the initial values of global variables.
Setting a pre-condition automatically fixes logical bugs in code.

q: Compare the standard Searching and Sorting algorithms (Binary Search, Linear Search, Bubble Sort, Selection Sort).
right: Binary Search requires the data to be sorted beforehand.
Linear Search has a time complexity that grows linearly with data size.
Bubble Sort compares adjacent elements and swaps them if needed.
Selection Sort finds the minimum value and moves it to the start.
Binary Search is significantly faster than Linear Search for large datasets.
Bubble Sort is generally considered inefficient for very large lists.
wrong: Binary Search starts from the first element and checks each one.
Selection Sort is a 'Divide and Conquer' algorithm.
Linear Search requires the array to be in alphabetical order.
Bubble Sort is the most efficient sorting algorithm available.
Binary Search works by comparing the target to the last element.
A 2D array cannot be sorted using a Bubble Sort algorithm.

q: What are the core principles and benefits of 'Thinking Procedurally' and 'Modularity' in software development?
right: It involves breaking down a complex problem into smaller, manageable sub-problems.
Sub-programmes (functions/procedures) allow for code reusability across a project.
It makes debugging easier because errors can be isolated to a single module.
Modular code is easier to maintain and update without breaking the entire system.
It allows multiple programmers to work on different parts of a system simultaneously.
Parameters allow data to be passed into sub-programs to perform specific tasks.
wrong: It requires all code to be written in a single, continuous main loop.
Thinking procedurally means the code can only run in a linear, top-down fashion.
Using sub-programmes significantly increases the total memory usage of an app.
Global variables are preferred over parameters to keep modules connected.
Modularity is only useful for projects that exceed 10,000 lines of code.
It eliminates the need for documenting code since each part is small.

q: Which of the following are true regarding the manipulation and structure of 2D Arrays (Matrices) in IB CS?
right: A 2D array can be visualized as a table with rows and columns.
Nested loops (a loop inside another loop) are used to traverse all elements.
The element at row 2, column 3 is typically accessed via DATA[2][3].
They are static data structures with a fixed number of rows and columns.
A 2D array can store a grid for a game, like Tic-Tac-Toe or Minesweeper.
Data in a 2D array must usually be of the same data type.
wrong: You can add new columns to a 2D array dynamically during runtime.
The .getNext() method is the standard way to navigate a 2D array.
A 2D array is a non-linear data structure, similar to a linked list.
Rows and columns must always have the same number of elements (square).
The outer loop always represents the columns in every programming language.
They use less memory than a Collection because they don't store pointers.

q: Identify the logical requirements and characteristics of 'Thinking Logically' and 'Decision Making' in algorithms.
right: Selection statements (IF-THEN-ELSE) allow for branching paths in logic.
Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) are used to evaluate complex conditions.
Decision making is often based on the comparison of variables or constants.
Iteration (loops) can be controlled by a boolean condition (WHILE loops).
Truth tables can be used to verify the logic of a complex conditional statement.
Algorithms must account for 'edge cases' where input data is at the limit.
wrong: An 'ELSE' statement can exist without an 'IF' statement.
Logical decisions are only made at the very start of an algorithm.
An 'AND' operation returns true if at least one of the conditions is met.
Thinking logically prevents the need for any iterative structures.
Nested 'IF' statements are considered a syntax error in IB pseudocode.
The 'NOT' operator flips a boolean value from true to true.

q: What are the specific requirements for a Binary Search to function correctly and efficiently?
right: The array must be sorted in ascending or descending order first.
It uses a 'Divide and Conquer' approach by halving the search area.
It compares the target value to the middle element of the current range.
It is much more efficient than a Linear Search for large datasets.
The variables 'low', 'high', and 'mid' are commonly used to track indices.
If the target is smaller than the middle, the search continues in the left half.
wrong: It starts the search from the first index and moves one by one.
Binary search can only be performed on Collections using .getNext().
It is the best choice for searching an unsorted list of names.
It will always find the target in fewer than three steps.
The middle element is calculated by adding the target value to the length.
It requires a 2D array to map the data points correctly.

q: In the context of 'Thinking Concurrently', what does this concept involve in a computing environment?
right: It involves managing multiple tasks that start, run, and complete in overlapping time periods.
A common example is a web server handling multiple user requests at once.
It can lead to faster execution if the hardware has multiple processor cores.
It requires careful management of shared resources to avoid 'deadlocks'.
Operating systems use concurrency to run background tasks while a user works.
It involves breaking a task into sub-tasks that can be processed independently.
wrong: It means the computer performs only one instruction at a time, very quickly.
Concurrency is only possible on computers with a single-core CPU.
It guarantees that every task will finish at exactly the same microsecond.
It removes the need for an Operating System's scheduler.
Concurrency and Parallelism are exactly the same concept in all contexts.
A standard 'FOR' loop is the primary tool for implementing concurrency.

q: When creating and using 'Trace Tables' to test an algorithm, what are the essential rules?
right: Every variable in the algorithm should have its own column in the table.
A new row is used whenever a variable's value changes.
It is used to track the state of an algorithm line-by-line.
The 'Output' column records any data displayed to the user.
It is an effective way to find 'Logic Errors' that don't crash the program.
The initial values of variables should be recorded in the first data row.
wrong: A trace table is only used for code that contains syntax errors.
You only need to record the final values of the variables.
Trace tables are generated automatically by the compiler during execution.
Loops should be skipped in a trace table to save space.
The table must include the physical memory address of each variable.
Variables that don't change their value should be left out of the table.

q: What are the specific operational steps and characteristics of the Selection Sort algorithm?
right: It divides the list into a sorted and an unsorted sub-list.
In each pass, it finds the smallest element in the unsorted sub-list.
The identified minimum element is swapped with the first element of the unsorted part.
After each pass, the sorted sub-list grows by exactly one element.
It performs $N-1$ passes to fully sort a list of $N$ items.
It is generally more efficient than Bubble Sort because it performs fewer swaps.
wrong: It compares every element to a 'pivot' value to split the list.
It repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order.
It is a recursive 'Divide and Conquer' algorithm like Merge Sort.
It requires a secondary temporary array to hold the sorted values.
The algorithm stops early if no swaps are made during a pass.
It can only be used on numerical data, not on strings or characters.

q: Which of the following are standard rules and symbols used in Flowcharts for algorithm design?
right: Rectangles are used to represent processes or internal operations.
Diamonds are used for decision points with 'Yes/No' or 'True/False' exits.
Parallelograms represent Input and Output (I/O) operations.
Arrows indicate the 'flow of control' or direction of execution.
Ovals or 'pill' shapes are used to mark the Start and End of the algorithm.
Every decision diamond must have at least two outgoing flow lines.
wrong: Triangles are used to represent the start of a loop structure.
Circles are used to indicate where a variable is declared.
A rectangle can have three or more exit arrows based on a condition.
Flowlines can cross each other without any specific meaning.
Input operations must always be placed inside a diamond symbol.
A flowchart cannot contain a loop that goes back to a previous step.

q: In computer programming, what are the differences between Global and Local variables?
right: Local variables are declared inside a sub-programme and exist only there.
Global variables are accessible from any part of the entire program.
A local variable is destroyed (deleted from memory) once the function ends.
Using local variables helps prevent accidental data changes in other modules.
Global variables can lead to 'side effects' that make debugging difficult.
Two different functions can use the same local variable name without conflict.
wrong: Local variables can be accessed by any function in the program.
Global variables use less memory than local variables.
A global variable is only created when a specific function is called.
IB Pseudocode requires all variables to be global for efficiency.
Local variables must be declared at the very top of the main programme.
Changing a local variable automatically updates all global variables.

q: Which statements correctly describe the passing of Parameters and the use of Return values?
right: Parameters allow a sub-programme to receive data from the calling code.
A 'Return' statement sends a result back to the part of the code that called it.
Functions typically return a value, while procedures (sub-routines) may not.
Arguments are the actual values passed into a parameter when a function runs.
Passing by value means a copy of the data is sent, protecting the original.
Parameters make sub-programmes more flexible and reusable.
wrong: A function can return up to ten different values simultaneously.
Parameters can only be used to pass integer data types.
Once a value is returned, the sub-programme continues to run in the background.
You must use a global variable to get a result out of a function.
The name of the argument must be identical to the name of the parameter.
Parameters are only required if the sub-programme contains a loop.

q: What is the logic behind the Bubble Sort algorithm during its execution?
right: It compares pairs of adjacent elements throughout the list.
In an ascending sort, the largest element 'bubbles' to the end in the first pass.
It uses a 'swapped' flag to check if the list is already sorted.
The number of elements to be compared decreases by one after each pass.
It requires nested loops: one for passes and one for comparisons.
It is simple to implement but inefficient for large datasets.
wrong: It selects the smallest item and moves it to the front immediately.
It halves the array size with every comparison to save time.
It only works if the number of elements is an even number.
It is the fastest sorting algorithm for lists with millions of items.
Items are moved to their correct position in a single pass.
It uses a 'pointer' that can only move backward through the array.

q: Identify the standard features of 'Thinking Abstractly' in the context of problem-solving.
right: It involves removing unnecessary details to focus on important characteristics.
It allows a designer to create a model of a real-world system.
Using a variable name like 'PRICE' instead of a memory address is an abstraction.
It helps in identifying patterns that can be applied to different problems.
In a map, the scale and symbols are abstractions of the actual terrain.
Abstraction reduces complexity for the programmer and the end user.
wrong: It means making the code as complex and detailed as possible.
It involves focusing on the specific hardware brand being used.
Abstraction is only useful when writing code in Machine Language (Binary).
It prevents the use of sub-programmes or modularity.
Thinking abstractly is the same as 'Thinking Concurrently'.
It requires the programmer to explain every line of code to the user.

q: What are the characteristics and differences between Syntax, Logic, and Runtime errors?
right: Syntax errors occur when the code violates the grammar rules of the language.
Logic errors allow the program to run but produce incorrect or unexpected results.
Runtime errors occur while the program is executing, often crashing it.
A missing bracket or semicolon is a classic example of a syntax error.
Dividing a number by zero usually triggers a runtime error.
Logic errors are the most difficult to find because the compiler doesn't flag them.
wrong: A syntax error is only discovered after the program has been fully executed.
Logic errors prevent the source code from being compiled into machine code.
Runtime errors are caused by incorrect variable naming conventions.
All logic errors are automatically corrected by the operating system.
Syntax errors occur when the user enters the wrong password.
A runtime error is another name for a 'Thinking Abstractly' failure.

q: Identify the standard features of 'Pre-test' (WHILE) vs 'Post-test' (LOOP UNTIL) loops.
right: A WHILE loop checks the condition before executing the loop body.
A LOOP UNTIL loop always executes its body at least once.
A WHILE loop may never execute if the initial condition is false.
Post-test loops check the condition after the code block has run.
In a WHILE loop, the condition must be true for the loop to continue.
In a LOOP UNTIL, the loop stops as soon as the condition becomes true.
wrong: A WHILE loop is a post-test structure used for fixed iterations.
LOOP UNTIL is the only loop that can access a 2D array.
Pre-test loops are less efficient because they require more memory.
A WHILE loop must always run at least 10 times before stopping.
The condition in a post-test loop is checked before the variables are initialized.
A LOOP UNTIL loop can never result in an infinite loop.

q: What are the fundamental data types used in IB Pseudocode and their purposes?
right: Integer is used for whole numbers without decimal points.
Real (or Float) is used for numbers that contain fractional parts.
Boolean represents only two possible states: True or False.
String is a collection of characters used for text data.
Char is used to store a single character, like 'A' or '$'.
Arrays are used to store multiple items of the same data type under one name.
wrong: The Boolean type is used to store large decimal values.
An Integer can store the text 'Hello World' if it is short.
A String data type can only be used for numerical calculations.
The Real data type is used only for storing dates and times.
A Char data type can store a whole paragraph of text.
Collections are the only way to store Integer data types in IB.

q: In algorithm design, what is the purpose and logic of using a 'Temporary' (temp) variable?
right: It is essential for swapping the values of two variables.
It prevents data loss when overwriting one variable with another.
It acts as a 'holding bay' during a swap operation.
It is commonly used inside the inner loop of a Bubble Sort.
It allows a value to be 'backed up' before a calculation changes it.
Without it, setting A = B would result in the original value of A being lost.
wrong: A temp variable is required to start any WHILE loop.
It is used to permanently store data in the hard drive.
Temp variables are only used in recursive algorithms.
Using a temp variable increases the Big O complexity of an algorithm.
The IB requires all temp variables to be named 'TEMP_VAR_01'.
A temp variable is a special type of hardware register in the CPU.

q: What are the common strategies for finding the Maximum or Minimum value in an array?
right: Initialize the 'MAX' variable to the value of the first element in the array.
Iterate through the array starting from the second element.
Compare each element to the current 'MAX' value.
If the current element is greater than 'MAX', update 'MAX' with that element.
This algorithm uses a single loop, making it a linear time operation.
It works for both numerical data and alphabetical strings.
wrong: Initialize 'MAX' to 0, even if the array contains negative numbers.
The array must be sorted before you can find the maximum value.
You must use a nested loop to compare every element with every other element.
The maximum value is always found at index [0].
A Collection must be used instead of an array to find a minimum value.
Finding a minimum value requires the use of a Stack data structure.

q: Which of the following are benefits of using 'Sub-programmes' (Methods/Functions)?
right: They promote code reusability by allowing the same logic to be called multiple times.
They improve readability by giving a name to a specific block of logic.
They make debugging easier by isolating specific tasks into modules.
They allow different programmers to work on different parts of a system.
They reduce the overall size of the code by eliminating redundancy.
They can be tested independently before being integrated into the main program.
wrong: They make the code run faster because the CPU skips the main loop.
Using sub-programmes removes the need for any variables.
They are only used in languages that do not support loops.
A sub-programme cannot contain an IF statement.
They are used to hide the source code from the compiler.
Every sub-programme must be at least 50 lines long.

q: What are 'Sentinel Values' and how are they used in programming?
right: A sentinel value is a specific value used to signal the end of a data stream.
It is often used in loops where the number of inputs is not known in advance.
The value chosen must be one that cannot be confused with legitimate data.
For a list of positive ages, -1 might be used as a sentinel value.
The loop checks for the sentinel value in its condition to decide when to stop.
It prevents the program from reading past the end of a file or input.
wrong: A sentinel value is the same as the index of an array.
It is used to store the largest number found in a search.
Sentinel values are required for every FOR loop in IB pseudocode.
It is a value that is automatically generated by the operating system.
The sentinel value is always the number 0.
It is used to encrypt data before it is sent over a network.

q: When 'Thinking Logically', how do Boolean Operators (AND, OR, NOT) function?
right: AND returns true only if both conditions being compared are true.
OR returns true if at least one of the conditions is true.
NOT reverses the logic; True becomes False and vice versa.
They are used to create complex conditions in IF statements.
In pseudocode, brackets can be used to define the order of logical evaluation.
An expression like (X > 5 AND X < 10) checks if X is between 6 and 9.
wrong: The AND operator returns true if either condition is true.
The NOT operator is used to skip the next line of code.
Logical operators can only be used with Integer data types.
OR returns false if both conditions are true.
Using AND twice in a row will crash the program.
Logical operators are only evaluated during the compilation phase.

q: What are the key differences between Static and Dynamic data structures?
right: Static structures (like Arrays) have a fixed size determined at compile time.
Dynamic structures (like Collections) can grow or shrink during execution.
Static structures are usually more memory-efficient if the size is known.
Dynamic structures use pointers to link data elements together.
An Array is a static structure; a Linked List or Collection is dynamic.
Static structures allow for faster 'random access' via an index.
wrong: Static structures can only store text, while dynamic can store numbers.
Dynamic structures are always stored in the CPU cache.
A static structure is one that cannot be changed once the program starts.
Dynamic structures do not require any memory to be allocated.
You cannot use a loop to traverse a static data structure.
The IB only allows the use of dynamic structures in Paper 1.

q: What is the purpose of the 'Modulo' (%) and 'Integer Division' (div) operators?
right: Modulo returns the remainder of a division (e.g., 10 % 3 = 1).
Integer Division returns only the whole number part of a division (e.g., 10 div 3 = 3).
Modulo is commonly used to check if a number is even or odd (X % 2 == 0).
Integer Division is useful for converting total minutes into hours.
They are essential for algorithms involving base conversion or cycles.
Modulo can be used to keep an array index within bounds (cycling).
wrong: Modulo is used to calculate the square root of a number.
Integer Division always returns a Real (decimal) number.
Modulo only works with negative numbers in IB Pseudocode.
Integer Division is the same as the standard '/' division symbol.
Using modulo on a string will reverse the characters.
These operators can only be used inside the 'Thinking Ahead' phase.

q: Identify characteristics of 'Internal Documentation' in program development.
right: It consists of comments written inside the source code.
It explains the purpose of complex logic or specific variables.
It helps other programmers understand and maintain the code.
It is ignored by the compiler and does not affect performance.
It includes meaningful variable names that clarify the code's intent.
It provides information about the author and the date of last update.
wrong: It is a printed manual given to the end-user of the software.
It is used to store the program's passwords securely.
Internal documentation makes the compiled program run slower.
It is only required for code that contains errors.
It is written in Binary so the computer can understand it.
It replaces the need for writing actual code logic.

q: What are the rules for 'Variable Naming' (Identifiers) in good programming practice?
right: Names should be descriptive of the data they hold (e.g., 'studentName').
They should follow a consistent convention like camelCase or snake_case.
Names cannot usually start with a number.
Reserved keywords (like 'IF' or 'WHILE') cannot be used as names.
They should not contain spaces or special characters like '!'.
Consistency in naming makes the code easier for others to read.
wrong: Variables should always be named with a single letter to save memory.
Every variable must start with an uppercase letter in IB pseudocode.
Spaces are allowed in variable names if they are inside quotes.
You should change the variable name every time its value changes.
Mathematical symbols like '+' can be used inside a variable name.
The compiler uses the length of the name to determine its data type.
